period-1/abassid-caliphate.html.title: Abassid Caliphate
period-1/abassid-caliphate.html.description: The major ARAB caliphate, during its reign was the Golden Age of Islam. Slowly, it did lose power in the Middle east but the final blow was only when the Mongols arrived and sacked Baghdad, turning it into the il-Khanate. It supported many centers of cultural learning such as Baghdad and the House of Wisdom, and the growth of trade was supported by these cultural and intellectual hubs that attracted merchants. After their fall, they would be replaced by the Turks, but their influence in creating such strong cultural sites still remained, especially in the technologies that H.O.W scholars developed.

period-1/al-andalus.html.title: Al-Andalus
period-1/al-andalus.html.description: Al-Andalus, by itself wasn't a huge power, but was the essential transfer point for how the ideas of Dar-al Islam transferred onto W. Europe, especially during the Crusades. Serving as this connection point, al-Andalus helped bring out ideas into the wider world and disperse goods & technology into Western Europe, helping create the basis for the Renaissance.

period-1/caravanserai.html.title: Caravanserai
period-1/caravanserai.html.description:
Secure rest stops and inns that helped secure the trade routes, making long-distance trading much easier. Similar to the Yam System, see for more details on why improved security helped
Another effect that occurred with the rise of caravanserai was that traders not only could rest and trade but they could also connect with one another, helping increase cross-cultural connections as merchants met in caravanserai (see people like Ibn Battuta)

period-1/censorate.html.title: Censorate
period-1/censorate.html.description: An investigative force of the chinese empire, it was mainly a conservative force, serving to put fear into bureaucrats and it would prevent radical reforms, persisting until the overthrow of the Qing dynasty. It would help investigate corruption in many of the ministries, but was mainly used to keep the Scholar-officials in check and in fear, helping China maintain power.

period-1/champa-rice.html.title: Champa Rice
period-1/champa-rice.html.description:
Champa rice was a strain of rice that allowed China's population to increase heavily, and it was gained by the tributary system in one of Vietnam's tributes.
Champa rice as a good has no other comparison, as it was a critical bulk food source. While other important crops are like “cash crops”, such as spices, the closest thing you can get to Champa rice, a good that actually directly improves living standards is Bananas, yet Champa rice drove China's population so much more than bananas did for their respective states.
This helped increase China's productivity during this time, and increased economic wealth helped lead to a stable and happy population, legitimizing their rule, especially important to maintain the Mandate Of Heaven

period-1/confucianism.html.title: Confucianism
period-1/confucianism.html.description:
A belief system that heavily influenced chinese thinking, this is HUGE and contributes to the overall trajectory of China - HUGELY IMPORTANT!
Respecting one's elders: a fundamental part of Chinese and Confucianist society. Filial piety helped reinforce the 5 Relationships and the clear social structure of Chinese society, making it even more difficult to rebel and leave one's social class as it acts as a cultural tie to maintain social hierarchies.
The 5 key relationships: husband-wife, peer-peer, ruler-subject, father-son, elder-younger sibling, were core relationships and had clear superior inferior interactions in Confucianism. This helped create a social hierarchy, making it easier for a ruler to govern. Another effect is in gender roles, where it developed and created a patriarchal society which can be seen through acts such as Foot Binding.Meritocratic examinations that anyone could take, leading to the creation of a scholar-official bureaucratic class. However, it was still unbalanced and unfair due to tutoring, much like the SAT and other "standardized" exams today, rich families could afford the tutors necessary, but sometimes poor towns would sponsor someone. By allowing limited social ability, rulers gave commoners a way to improve their status keeping commoners more accepting of their lowered social status (they "deserved" it). However, the prohibitive cost also served to still keep commoners out of higher social levels, and helped keep the rulers in power.
The most important thing was that in BOTH the Ming and Song dynasties, Confucianism was a continuity. This continuity is important because it shows us how even in the face of the Mongols, China itself changed little to accommodate for them, culturally and socially, and the major changes were economic and political. The "heart" of China stayed the same.

period-1/golden-horde.html.title: Golden Horde
period-1/golden-horde.html.description:
The Khanate of the Golden Horde was the name that the Russians gave to the Mongols, and controlled Russia. This Khanate allowed princes to be autonomous, and there was little impact politically and culturally - there was the least assimilation on the Mongol's part here. However, by letting princes be autonomous, they weakened their own control and made it easier to rebel as princes gained centralized power and gained wealth.
Another effect of their actions by staying hands-off was the survival of the Eastern Orthodox Church, which they did NOT destroy.

period-1/grand-canal.html.title: Grand Canal
period-1/grand-canal.html.description: While the Grand Canal itself was built and predated the Song dynasty, during the Song dynasty they extended the Canal and made a lot of general improvements to the grand canal, helping control floods and also increase agricultural productivity. This helps China improve and helps them maintain their rule as this economic improvement helps stability. Combined with a highly commercialized economy, the increased Grand Canal allowed for trade and huge economic output, leading to the so-called Song Economic Revolution. All of this helped legitimize their government and helped them keep the Mandate Of Heaven

period-1/il-khanate.html.title: il-Khanate
period-1/il-khanate.html.description: The Persian Khanate of the Mongols, after the sacking of Baghdad, they destroyed the Abassid Caliphate and ended the Arab rule of Islam (leading to the rise of the Turks). The Mongols would assimilate into Muslim/Persian culture and adapt their customs to maintain their rule. The formation of the il-Khanate was significant and changed politics in the Middle East due to their ending of the Abassid caliphate and the ensuing power vacuum leading to the rise of the Turks in Islam, such as the Mamluks.

period-1/islam.html.title: Islam
period-1/islam.html.description: Islam is one of the main Abrahamic religions, and is relatively recent. It has a lot of traits and terminology... as a basic rundown, Muslims believe in parts of other Abrahamic religions (Judaism and Christianity) but ultimately believe that these have diverged and hold their own holy scripture, the Quran, as truth. They follow the Sharia law, religious law that governs all aspects of law. This basic aspect of Muslim nations is part of what makes Islam appealing to adopt by leaders due to the strict laws it already has built into it. Another important aspect of Muslims are the Sufis, religious missionaries, who are extremely important during this time to spread Muslim thought and Islam.

period-1/malaccan-sultanate.html.title: Malaccan Sultanate
period-1/malaccan-sultanate.html.description:
The strait of Malacca was a hugely important strategic strait that controlled the S.E asia trade route with China and India. Notably, the Malaccan Sultanate was a Muslim sultanate, demonstrating the reach of Islam during this time. They also demonstrate the extent of Sufis, Muslim missionaries, who brought Islam to the Spice Islands.
Similar to the Swahili Trade States, the Malaccan Sultanate was trade-based state, focusing on taxing trade through the strait in order to generate profit to fund its economy and military. The Malaccan Sultanate demonstrates that trade could become the basis of a nation as networks of exchange developed during this time period.

period-1/mali-empire.html.title: Mali Empire
period-1/mali-empire.html.description: The gold empire of West Africa in this time period, replaced by  the Songhai empire BUT because of Mansa Musa’s efforts, their Muslim ideas were continued to the next empires, and thus Africa adopted Islam.The Mali empire, similar to the idea of the Pax Mongolica, created a similar Pax Mandinka, where the Mali empire sponsored trade and allowed trade to flourish. Similar to the Yam System, the Mali empire deterred bandits and secured trade routes.

period-1/mandate-of-heaven.html.title: Mandate Of Heaven
period-1/mandate-of-heaven.html.description:
The parallel of Divine Right of Kings in China, the mandate of heaven essentially stated that dynasties had the right to rule and also the right to be overthrown as soon as they became tyrannic. Thus, most dynasties would attempt to use legitimacy and not direct control in order to maintain power over their population, knowing that direct control would lead to a rebellion.
However, this also helped them, as with economic growth and a commercialized economy the Song would be able to legitimize themselves for centuries due to their economic revolution.

period-1/mansa-musa.html.title: Mansa Musa
period-1/mansa-musa.html.description: The richest man during this time, mainly remembered for his expensive Hajj, where he made his pilgrimage to Mecca. It was ridiculously expensive, where Mansa Musa threw money and gold everywhere, additionally building Mosques, contributing to the spread of Islam in Africa. Mansa Musa was a devout Muslim, and after completing his Hajj, he was even more devout and essentially converted the entirety of the Mali empire into a Muslim state. This Hajj helped legitimize the Mali Empire by demonstrating the immense wealth of the Mali empire. As a secondary goal, it also led to the spread of Islam in Africa as well, cementing African empires in the use of Islam to maintain power. Another effect of his hajj was the legends it created, leading Europeans to flock to Africa to try and find wealth (the Catalan Atlas and that one image)

period-1/muslim-berbers.html.title: Muslim Berbers
period-1/muslim-berbers.html.description:
Salt traders who were mostly nomadic, serving as the intermediaries between the Mediterranean and the rest of the Sub-Saharan trade network.
They mainly just serve as another example of a group of people in Dar al-Islam trading.

period-1/pax-mongolica.html.title: Pax Mongolica
period-1/pax-mongolica.html.description: The Pax Mongolica was a period of peace under the Mongols in which the Mongols secured the Silk Road with the use of the Yam system. These relays helped secure the trade routes and made it much safer and cheaper to trade. Additionally, the expansion of the Mongol empire meant that many regions like Russia, China, Persia, and parts of Europe were now interconnected. Under the Pax Mongolica, trade was encouraged and incentivized, leading to the growth of trade across the giant Mongol empire. The state encouragement of trade and the deliberate securing of trade routes led to expanded networks of exchange. This peace was a MASSIVE change, due to the immense scale that the Mongols aimed to conduct trade at - never before had previously disconnected regions interacted at such a large scale. Before, ideas, culture, goods, and technology only moved through a single region, but after the Mongols connected these regions (Persia, Europe, china, etc), ideas, technology, etc. moved across regions (examples could be gunpowder moving further just China to Europe, compass, etc.)

The securing of the Mongol empire's trade routes, leading to the Pax Mongolica. These relays made it much safer and cheaper for trade to occur, and these relays helped with communication as well and governance over their massive empire. The relays did not govern trade, instead they secured the trade routes and only taxed merchants using their services slightly - by not controlling trade, and only taxing merchants, trade still occurred and the increased safety overweighed the cost. Thus, the Yam System, by securing trade routes, helped expand the use of trade networks and due to the size of the Mongol empire, helped facilitate cross-regional interactions.Yam SystemSecure rest stops and inns that helped secure the trade routes, making long-distance trading much easier.
Similar to the Yam System, see for more details on why improved security helped

The Malaccan Sultanate demonstrates that trade could become the basis of a nation as networks of exchange developed during this time period.Malaccan SultanateIndia
The Persian Khanate of the Mongols, after the sacking of Baghdad, they destroyed the Abassid Caliphate and ended the Arab rule of Islam (leading to the rise of the Turks). The Mongols would assimilate into Muslim/Persian culture and adapt their customs to maintain their rule. The formation of the il-Khanate was significant and changed politics in the Middle East due to their ending of the Abassid caliphate and the ensuing power vacuum leading to the rise of the Turks in Islam, such as the Mamluks.il-KhanateThe Khanate of the Golden Horde was the name that the Russians gave to the Mongols, and controlled Russia. This Khanate allowed princes to be autonomous, and there was little impact politically and culturally - there was the least assimilation on the Mongol's part here. However, by letting princes be autonomous, they weakened their own control and made it easier to rebel as princes gained centralized power and gained wealth. Another effect of their actions by staying hands-off was the survival of the Eastern Orthodox Church, which they did NOT destroy.Golden HordeThe dynasty that the Mongols created in China, to legitimize themselves as rules while they extracted wealth from China - keep in mind this is a dynasty, they had to follow dynastic rules. In the Yuan Dynasty, the Mongols sinicized, and didn’t cause major changes to China - the Yuan dynasty adopted Confucian traditions, continuing typical Chinese dynastic traditions. They would follow many Confucian traditions in order to maintain their power and legitimize themselves, ultimately staying out of China. China changed the Mongols, but the Mongols didn’t change China.Yuan Dynasty
The parallel of Divine Right of Kings in China, the mandate of heaven essentially stated that dynasties had the right to rule and also the right to be overthrown as soon as they became tyrannic. Thus, most dynasties would attempt to use legitimacy and not direct control in order to maintain power over their population, knowing that direct control would lead to a rebellion.
However, this also helped them, as with economic growth and a commercialized economy the Song would be able to legitimize themselves for centuries due to their economic revolution. Mandate Of HeavenoaGender Parallelism
An investigative force of the chinese empire, it was mainly a conservative force, serving to put fear into bureaucrats and it would prevent radical reforms, persisting until the overthrow of the Qing dynasty. It would help investigate corruption in many of the ministries, but was mainly used to keep the Scholar-officials in check and in fear, helping China maintain power.Censorate
China gained immense wealth by forcing other nations to pay tribute to them, in goods or money (goods like Champa Rice were accepted as well). They mainly had three major tributary states: Vietnam, Japan, and Korea. By forcing other states to pay tribute, China was able to maintain power and prevent the other states from accumulating too much wealth. The tribute system was based on the idea that China is the cultural, economic, and technological leader of Asia, and as long as tribute states kept paying tribute, that idea was perpetuated and legitimized China’s rule.
An effect of this was that China would sinicize other nations and cause them to change.
Korea would basically become a Chinese clone, adopting Confucianism entirely, and Neo-Confucianism was adopted by Japan and Korea. The Chinese Writing system was adopted by all three as well.
Furthermore, in Japan and Korea (Vietnam resisted), the role of women also shifted due to Confucianism, and these nations became more patriarchal.Tribute SystemChina
The Zheng He voyages, during the Ming empire, were voyages to establish trade relations that expanded China’s view of the world, but these voyages were stopped after Zheng He - China isolated itself (Due to Ming Xenophobia & confucianism). However, these voyages were extremely successful, going as far as many of the Swahili Trade States in East Africa.
The Ming dynasty was prosperous at this time and thus the voyages were extravagant, with ships of sizes never seen before. This demonstrated to their tributary states and their population how prosperous and powerful they were, intimidating and also convincing their population not to rebel due to their prosperity/strength. These voyages helped legitimize the Ming Dynasty, yet they were shut down due to confucianist elites (yes I know that's not a word) fearing that this extended view would lead to more radical views from their populationZheng He VoyagesAztecsIncanCamels were the primary beast of burden, and were necessary to traverse the Saharan desert due to their capacity to stay alive and not die, unlike horses. Without camels, it would be impossible to develop the sub-saharan trade networks to the scale they existed at, as large-scale trade would not be possible.Camels only became useful after the invention of camel-saddles, facilitating trade in the Sahara as they could finally be used as true beasts of burden to carry loads, not just people. Without the camel-saddle and the camel, large transactions, especially transactions of SALT, which was vital to the Saharan economy.
Knowledge of the Saharan environment and the existence of guides was also tremendously important, for example in the case of Ibn Battuta, we know that guides were common and widespread. Due to the harsh nature of the Sahara, it would have been suicide to attempt a crossing without knowledge of oasises and other intermediate destinations, and as knowledge was accumulated, it led to the growth of trade networks as merchants found it more safe to conduct business.Sub-Saharan Technological InnovationsIslam is one of the main Abrahamic religions, and is relatively recent. It has a lot of traits and terminology... as a basic rundown, Muslims believe in parts of other Abrahamic religions (Judaism and Christianity) but ultimately believe that these have diverged and hold their own holy scripture, the Quran, as truth. They follow the Sharia law, religious law that governs all aspects of law. This basic aspect of Muslim nations is part of what makes Islam appealing to adopt by leaders due to the strict laws it already has built into it.
Another important aspect of Muslims are the Sufis, religious missionaries, who are extremely important during this time to spread Muslim thought and Islam.Islam
As a highly important cultural hotpost, it was developed after Mansa Musa's Hajj, extremely Muslim, and was a critical trade city. Similar to Baghdad, as a core cultural hotspot, it also served as an important place of learning.
Timbuktu also served a practical purpose of legitimizing the Mali Empire, by serving as a beacon of its strength and Muslim identity. Thus, it attracted many other traders from other nations justifying the Mali empire to both foreign and domestic peoples.TimbuktuThe richest man during this time, mainly remembered for his expensive Hajj, where he made his pilgrimage to Mecca. It was ridiculously expensive, where Mansa Musa threw money and gold everywhere, additionally building Mosques, contributing to the spread of Islam in Africa. Mansa Musa was a devout Muslim, and after completing his Hajj, he was even more devout and essentially converted the entirety of the Mali empire into a Muslim state.
This Hajj helped legitimize the Mali Empire by demonstrating the immense wealth of the Mali empire. As a secondary goal, it also led to the spread of Islam in Africa as well, cementing African empires in the use of Islam to maintain power. Another effect of his hajj was the legends it created, leading Europeans to flock to Africa to try and find wealth (the Catalan Atlas and that one image)Mansa Musa

headers.heading: Trade,  growing networks of exhange, and their effects are the most important things to know in P1!

period-1/period-1-home.html.title: Period 1 Home
period-1/period-1-home.html.description:
The 1200-1450 was dominated by the effects of increasing trade There were many wide-reaching effects, such as the spread of religions, bubonic plague, increased inter-community interactions During this time period, Europe was far behind and Dar al-Islam/China were the major powers during this time The IOB was by far the most important trading area In the Americas, the Aztecs and Inca set up their own empires/states which operated to some extent similarly to the Afro-Eurasian empires
The three major trade routes were the Sub-Saharan trade routes, the Silk Road, and the IOB (maritime)
With rising trade, so did rising production of luxury goods from India and China that increased their power

period-1/song-agri-techniques.html.title: Song Agri Techniques
period-1/song-agri-techniques.html.description:
China developed many methods in order to increase agricultural production, such as irrigation, terrace farming, and crop rotation. These methods helped its population explode and made farming very efficient, and would be transferred to the European states.
This helped China legitimize their rule is by increasing prosperity, since a prosperous nation is one that is less likely to have rebellions. China was able to be stable during the Song dynasty since there was a food surplus to power population growth without starvation. This stability allowed the Song dynasty to maintain its rule and their Mandate Of Heaven
Eventually, these techniques would spread throughout the SIlk Road, and by not having to live hand to mouth, Commercialization occured on the global scale, improving the entire world economy.

period-1/sub-saharan-technological-innov.title: Sub-Saharan Technological Innovations
period-1/sub-saharan-technological-innov.description: Camels were the primary beast of burden, and were necessary to traverse the Saharan desert due to their capacity to stay alive and not die, unlike horses. Without camels, it would be impossible to develop the sub-saharan trade networks to the scale they existed at, as large-scale trade would not be possible.Camels only became useful after the invention of camel-saddles, facilitating trade in the Sahara as they could finally be used as true beasts of burden to carry loads, not just people. Without the camel-saddle and the camel, large transactions, especially transactions of SALT, which was vital to the Saharan economy.Knowledge of the Saharan environment and the existence of guides was also tremendously important, for example in the case of Ibn Battuta, we know that guides were common and widespread. Due to the harsh nature of the Sahara, it would have been suicide to attempt a crossing without knowledge of oasises and other intermediate destinations, and as knowledge was accumulated, it led to the growth of trade networks as merchants found it more safe to conduct business.

period-1/swahili-trade-states.html.title: Swahili Trade States
period-1/swahili-trade-states.html.description: The so-called lands of the Zanj, city states like Mogadishu, Kilwa, and many other states. They are a clear example of trade based states! Similar to other nations such as the Mali Empire, the Muslim Berbers, Al-Andalus, the Malaccan Sultanate, etc. these states sponsored trade heavily to build their economies. Helped heavily to expand IOB trade to Africa and connect IOB trade to the Sub-Saharan trade network. They utilized trade heavily in order to survive, with the Zanj states being decentralized states that competed with each other for trade. With so much competition, trade increased even more as trade became more profitable as each state had to lower prices (supply and demand).

period-1/timbuktu.html.title: Timbuktu
period-1/timbuktu.html.description:
As a highly important cultural hotpost, it was developed after Mansa Musa's Hajj, extremely Muslim, and was a critical trade city. Similar to Baghdad, as a core cultural hotspot, it also served as an important place of learning.
Timbuktu also served a practical purpose of legitimizing the Mali Empire, by serving as a beacon of its strength and Muslim identity. Thus, it attracted many other traders from other nations justifying the Mali empire to both foreign and domestic peoples.

period-1/tribute-system.html.title: Tribute System
period-1/tribute-system.html.description:
China gained immense wealth by forcing other nations to pay tribute to them, in goods or money (goods like Champa Rice were accepted as well). They mainly had three major tributary states: Vietnam, Japan, and Korea. By forcing other states to pay tribute, China was able to maintain power and prevent the other states from accumulating too much wealth. The tribute system was based on the idea that China is the cultural, economic, and technological leader of Asia, and as long as tribute states kept paying tribute, that idea was perpetuated and legitimized China’s rule.
An effect of this was that China would sinicize other nations and cause them to change.
Korea would basically become a Chinese clone, adopting Confucianism entirely, and Neo-Confucianism was adopted by Japan and Korea. The Chinese Writing system was adopted by all three as well.
Furthermore, in Japan and Korea (Vietnam resisted), the role of women also shifted due to Confucianism, and these nations became more patriarchal.

period-1/untitled.html.title: Untitled

period-1/yam-system.html.title: Yam System
period-1/yam-system.html.description: The securing of the Mongol empire's trade routes, leading to the Pax Mongolica. These relays made it much safer and cheaper for trade to occur, and these relays helped with communication as well and governance over their massive empire. The relays did not govern trade, instead they secured the trade routes and only taxed merchants using their services slightly - by not controlling trade, and only taxing merchants, trade still occurred and the increased safety overweighed the cost. Thus, the Yam System, by securing trade routes, helped expand the use of trade networks and due to the size of the Mongol empire, helped facilitate cross-regional interactions.

period-1/yuan-dynasty.html.title: Yuan Dynasty
period-1/yuan-dynasty.html.description:
The dynasty that the Mongols created in China, to legitimize themselves as rules while they extracted wealth from China - keep in mind this is a dynasty, they had to follow dynastic rules.
In the Yuan Dynasty, the Mongols sinicized, and didn’t cause major changes to China - the Yuan dynasty adopted Confucian traditions, continuing typical Chinese dynastic traditions. They would follow many Confucian traditions in order to maintain their power and legitimize themselves, ultimately staying out of China. China changed the Mongols, but the Mongols didn’t change China.

period-1/zheng-he-voyages.html.title: Zheng He Voyages
period-1/zheng-he-voyages.html.description:
The Zheng He voyages, during the Ming empire, were voyages to establish trade relations that expanded China’s view of the world, but these voyages were stopped after Zheng He - China isolated itself (Due to Ming Xenophobia & confucianism). However, these voyages were extremely successful, going as far as many of the Swahili Trade States in East Africa.
The Ming dynasty was prosperous at this time and thus the voyages were extravagant, with ships of sizes never seen before. This demonstrated to their tributary states and their population how prosperous and powerful they were, intimidating and also convincing their population not to rebel due to their prosperity/strength. These voyages helped legitimize the Ming Dynasty, yet they were shut down due to confucianist elites (yes I know that's not a word) fearing that this extended view would lead to more radical views from their population

info_defaultFolderIcon.description: The icon to use for folders. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultFileIcon.description: The icon to use for files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultMediaIcon.description: The icon to use for media files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded

info_siteUrl.description: The url that this site will be hosted at

info_authorName.description: The name of the author of the site
